title: How to Visit a Doctor in Japan
summary: What to bring, what to expect, and how the process works at a Japanese clinic

steps.title: Find a clinic near you
steps.detail: Search for a nearby clinic (kurinikku) or hospital (byouin). Smaller clinics handle most general visits. No appointment needed at many clinics — walk-ins are common.
steps.tip: Google Maps works well. Search 'clinic' or 'internal medicine' (naika) near your location.

steps.title: Bring your insurance card
steps.detail: Your National Health Insurance (NHI) card or employer-provided health insurance card (hokenshou) is required. NHI covers 70% of costs — you pay 30%.
steps.tip: If you forget your card, you pay 100% upfront. Return with the card within the same month to get a refund.

steps.title: Arrive and fill out the intake form
steps.detail: At reception, hand over your insurance card. You will receive a monshin-hyou (intake questionnaire). Fill in your symptoms, allergies, and current medications.
steps.tip: Many clinics have English forms. If not, show your symptoms written on your phone.

steps.title: Wait to be called
steps.detail: Wait in the waiting room. Clinics call patients by name or number. Wait times range from 15 minutes to over an hour depending on the clinic.
steps.tip: Bring something to read. Popular clinics can have long waits, especially Monday mornings.

steps.title: See the doctor
steps.detail: The doctor will examine you and explain the diagnosis. Consultations are typically brief (5-10 minutes). The doctor may write a prescription (shohousen).
steps.tip: Prepare your symptoms in simple Japanese or English. Many doctors understand basic medical English.

steps.title: Pay at reception
steps.detail: After your consultation, pay at the reception desk. With insurance, a typical clinic visit costs 1,000-3,000 yen. Cash is preferred; some clinics accept cards.
steps.tip: Keep your receipt (ryoushuu-sho) for tax deduction purposes if your annual medical expenses exceed 100,000 yen.

steps.title: Pick up your prescription
steps.detail: Take your prescription slip (shohousen) to a nearby pharmacy (yakkyoku). The pharmacy fills the prescription and you pay the medication cost (also 30% with insurance).
steps.tip: Pharmacies near clinics are convenient but any pharmacy in Japan will accept your prescription.

phrases.ja: 初診です
phrases.en: This is my first visit

phrases.ja: 予約はありません
phrases.en: I do not have an appointment

phrases.ja: 熱があります
phrases.en: I have a fever

phrases.ja: 頭が痛いです
phrases.en: I have a headache

phrases.ja: 英語を話せる先生はいますか
phrases.en: Is there a doctor who speaks English?
phrases.romaji: eigo wo hanaseru sensei wa imasu ka

title: How to Call 119 (Ambulance / Fire)
summary: Japan's emergency number for ambulance and fire. Free to call, available 24/7

steps.title: Dial 119 from any phone
steps.detail: Call 119 from any phone (mobile or landline). This is the emergency number for both ambulance (kyuukyuu) and fire (kaji). The call is free.
steps.tip: For police, call 110 instead. 119 is only for medical emergencies and fire.

steps.title: Say 'kyuukyuusha onegaishimasu'
steps.detail: When the operator answers, say 'kyuukyuusha onegaishimasu' (ambulance please) for medical emergencies. For fire, say 'kaji desu' (it's a fire).
steps.tip: Some areas have multilingual operators. If you cannot speak Japanese, say 'English please' slowly.

steps.title: Give your location
steps.detail: The operator will ask for your address (juusho). Give the full address or nearest landmark. If outdoors, describe nearby buildings or intersections.
steps.tip: Check Google Maps for your exact address. Utility poles often have address plates (blue signs).

steps.title: Describe the situation
steps.detail: Explain what happened and the patient's condition: conscious or unconscious, breathing or not, visible injuries. The operator may give first aid instructions.
steps.tip: Stay calm and speak slowly. The operator is trained to guide you through the process.

steps.title: Wait for the ambulance
steps.detail: Average response time is 7-9 minutes in urban areas. Stay on the line if instructed. Go outside to guide the ambulance to the correct location if possible.
steps.tip: Ambulances in Japan are free. You will not be charged for the ambulance ride.

steps.title: At the hospital
steps.detail: The ambulance crew will take the patient to the nearest available hospital. Bring the patient's insurance card and ID if possible. The hospital will treat first, handle payment later.
steps.tip: Emergency treatment is provided regardless of insurance status. Settle payment afterward.

phrases.ja: 救急車お願いします
phrases.en: Ambulance please

phrases.ja: 火事です
phrases.en: It is a fire

phrases.ja: 意識がありません
phrases.en: They are unconscious

phrases.ja: 呼吸していません
phrases.en: They are not breathing

phrases.ja: 住所は〇〇です
phrases.en: The address is...

title: How to Find English-Speaking Clinics
summary: Resources and methods to find clinics and hospitals with English-speaking staff

steps.title: Check the AMDA Medical Information Center
steps.detail: AMDA International Medical Information Center provides multilingual medical consultation. Call 03-6233-9266 for help finding English-speaking doctors in your area.
steps.tip: AMDA operates Monday to Friday. Check their website for current hours.

steps.title: Search your city or ward website
steps.detail: Many city and ward offices maintain lists of medical facilities with foreign language support. Search '[your city] English speaking hospital' or check the international residents section of the municipal website.
steps.tip: Tokyo, Osaka, and other major cities have comprehensive multilingual medical facility lists.

steps.title: Use the JNTO Medical Facility Search
steps.detail: Japan National Tourism Organization (JNTO) maintains a searchable database of medical facilities that accept foreign patients, searchable by area and language.
steps.tip: This database is also useful for finding facilities that accept travel insurance.

steps.title: Ask your local international center
steps.detail: Most prefectures have international exchange associations (kokusai kouryuu kyoukai) that maintain referral lists for English-speaking medical professionals.
steps.tip: These centers often provide free interpretation services for medical visits.

steps.title: Check Google Maps reviews
steps.detail: Search Google Maps for clinics near you and read reviews in English. Other foreign residents often mention whether staff speak English.
steps.tip: Larger hospitals are more likely to have English-speaking staff than small neighborhood clinics.

steps.title: Call ahead to confirm
steps.detail: Before visiting, call the clinic and ask 'Eigo de shinryou dekimasu ka?' (Can I receive treatment in English?). This saves time and avoids miscommunication.
steps.tip: Even if a doctor does not speak fluent English, many can communicate basic medical information.

phrases.ja: 英語で診療できますか
phrases.en: Can I receive treatment in English?

phrases.ja: 通訳はいますか
phrases.en: Is there an interpreter?

phrases.ja: 英語を話せる先生はいますか
phrases.en: Is there a doctor who speaks English?
phrases.romaji: eigo wo hanaseru sensei wa imasu ka

title: How to Use NHI at a Pharmacy
summary: How to fill a prescription at a Japanese pharmacy with your health insurance

steps.title: Get your prescription slip from the doctor
steps.detail: After your clinic visit, the doctor gives you a prescription slip (shohousen). This is a paper form listing your medications. It is valid for 4 days including the date of issue.
steps.tip: Do not lose the prescription slip. If it expires, you need a new one from the doctor.

steps.title: Go to any pharmacy
steps.detail: Take the prescription to any pharmacy (yakkyoku) in Japan. You are not required to use a specific pharmacy. Pharmacies near clinics are convenient but often busy.
steps.tip: Look for the green cross sign or the kanji 薬局 (yakkyoku).

steps.title: Hand over your prescription and insurance card
steps.detail: Give the pharmacist your prescription slip and health insurance card. If it is your first visit to this pharmacy, you may fill out a brief medical history form.
steps.tip: The pharmacist may ask about allergies and other medications you take.

steps.title: Wait for your medication
steps.detail: The pharmacist prepares your medication. Wait time is usually 10-20 minutes. For common medications, it may be faster.
steps.tip: Some pharmacies offer to send a text notification when your order is ready.

steps.title: Receive your medication and explanation
steps.detail: The pharmacist explains how to take each medication: dosage, frequency, and timing (before/after meals). They provide a medication info sheet (yakuzai jouhou teikyousho).
steps.tip: Ask for English instructions if available. Take a photo of the Japanese instructions to translate later.

steps.title: Pay with your insurance
steps.detail: With NHI, you pay 30% of the medication cost. Cash is most common; some pharmacies accept cards or electronic payment. A typical prescription costs 500-2,000 yen.
steps.tip: Keep your pharmacy receipts. You receive a medication record book (okusuri techou) to track all prescriptions.

bring: Medication record book (okusuri techou) if you have one

phrases.ja: この処方箋をお願いします
phrases.en: I would like to fill this prescription

phrases.ja: お薬手帳はありますか
phrases.en: Do you have a medication record book?

phrases.ja: ジェネリックでお願いします
phrases.en: Generic version please

phrases.ja: 飲み方を教えてください
phrases.en: Please explain how to take this

title: How to Register at City Hall
summary: Residence registration is required within 14 days of moving. Here is how to do it.

steps.title: Go within 14 days of moving
steps.detail: By law, you must register your address at the municipal office (shiyakusho / kuyakusho) within 14 days of moving to a new address. This applies when you first arrive in Japan and every time you move.
steps.tip: Late registration may cause issues with insurance and other services. Do this as soon as possible.

steps.title: Prepare your documents
steps.detail: Bring your residence card (zairyuu card), passport, and the address of your new home. If moving within Japan, you also need the moving-out certificate (tenshutsu todoke) from your previous city hall.
steps.tip: Write your new address in Japanese before going. Your landlord or real estate agent can provide this.

steps.title: Go to the resident registration counter
steps.detail: At city hall, find the resident registration counter (juumin ka or shimin ka). Take a number and wait. Operating hours are typically weekday 8:30 AM to 5:00 PM.
steps.tip: Some city halls open on select Saturdays. Check your city hall website for hours.

steps.title: Fill out the moving-in notification
steps.detail: Complete the moving-in notification form (tennyu todoke). Staff can help you fill it out. You write your name, nationality, address, and residence card number.
steps.tip: Ask for help if you cannot read the form. Staff at city halls in major cities often handle foreign residents regularly.

steps.title: Submit and get your card updated
steps.detail: Hand in the form with your documents. The staff updates the address on the back of your residence card. This usually takes 15-30 minutes.
steps.tip: Your residence card is your official ID in Japan. Keep the address on it current at all times.

steps.title: Enroll in National Health Insurance (if applicable)
steps.detail: If you are not covered by employer insurance, enroll in NHI at the same time. This is done at the insurance counter (kokumin kenko hoken) in the same building.
steps.tip: NHI premiums are based on your previous year income. First-year residents often pay reduced premiums.

bring: My Number notification card (if you have one)

phrases.ja: 転入届を出したいです
phrases.en: I want to submit a moving-in notification

phrases.ja: 住民登録をしたいです
phrases.en: I want to register as a resident

phrases.ja: 国民健康保険に入りたいです
phrases.en: I want to enroll in National Health Insurance
phrases.romaji: kokumin kenkou hoken ni hairitai desu

phrases.ja: 英語対応はありますか
phrases.en: Is English support available?

title: How to Get a My Number Card
summary: The My Number card is a government-issued photo ID. Application takes 1-2 months.

steps.title: Receive your My Number notification
steps.detail: When you register at city hall, you receive a My Number (12-digit individual number). You also get a notification card (tsuuchi card) — a paper card with your number. This is NOT the My Number Card itself.
steps.tip: Keep your My Number confidential. Do not share it unnecessarily.

steps.title: Apply for the My Number Card
steps.detail: Apply at your city hall, online, or by mail. You need a photo (4.5cm x 3.5cm, white background, taken within 6 months). The online application is at mynumbercard.point.soumu.go.jp.
steps.tip: The online application is the fastest method. You can take the photo with your smartphone.

steps.title: Wait for the notification letter
steps.detail: Processing takes approximately 1-2 months. You will receive a pickup notification letter (koufuu tsuuchi-sho) by mail when your card is ready.
steps.tip: Processing times vary. Urban areas may be faster due to more frequent batches.

steps.title: Pick up your card at city hall
steps.detail: Bring the pickup notification letter, your notification card (tsuuchi card), your residence card, and a photo ID. Go to the designated counter at city hall.
steps.tip: You must pick up the card in person. A representative cannot collect it for you in most cases.

steps.title: Set your PINs
steps.detail: At pickup, you set two PINs: a 4-digit PIN for basic use and a 6-16 character password for digital certificate functions (e-Tax, etc.).
steps.tip: Remember your PINs. If you forget them, you must reset at city hall in person.

steps.title: Start using your card
steps.detail: The My Number Card works as a photo ID, can be used for tax filing (e-Tax), accessing government online services, and as a health insurance card (after registration).
steps.tip: You can link your My Number Card to your health insurance at city hall or online via the mynaportal.

phrases.ja: マイナンバーカードを申請したいです
phrases.en: I want to apply for a My Number Card
phrases.romaji: mainanbaa kaado wo shinsei shitai desu

phrases.ja: マイナンバーカードを受け取りに来ました
phrases.en: I came to pick up my My Number Card
phrases.romaji: mainanbaa kaado wo uketori ni kimashita

phrases.ja: 暗証番号を設定します
phrases.en: I will set my PIN

title: How to Renew Your Residence Card
summary: Residence card renewal process at the Immigration Services Agency

steps.title: Check your expiration date
steps.detail: Your residence card (zairyuu card) has an expiration date on the front. You can apply for renewal starting 3 months before expiration.
steps.tip: Do not let your card expire. Overstaying is a serious immigration violation.

steps.title: Prepare your documents
steps.detail: Required documents vary by visa type but generally include: application form, passport, current residence card, a 4cm x 3cm photo, and supporting documents for your visa category (employment certificate, school enrollment, etc.).
steps.tip: Check the Immigration Services Agency website for your specific visa category requirements.

steps.title: Go to the Immigration Services Bureau
steps.detail: Visit your regional Immigration Services Bureau (shutsunyuukoku zairyuu kanri kyoku). Major cities have offices in Tokyo (Shinagawa), Osaka, Nagoya, Fukuoka, etc. Arrive early as wait times can be very long.
steps.tip: Some regional offices are less crowded than major city offices. Check if a satellite office near you handles renewals.

steps.title: Submit your application
steps.detail: Take a number at the counter, submit your application and documents. The staff reviews your documents and issues a receipt. Processing typically takes 2-4 weeks.
steps.tip: If documents are missing, you may be asked to return with additional materials. Bring everything the first time.

steps.title: Receive your notification postcard
steps.detail: When your renewal is approved, you receive a postcard (hagaki) by mail. This postcard is your pickup notification.
steps.tip: If you do not receive the postcard within the expected timeframe, you can call to check status.

steps.title: Pick up your new residence card
steps.detail: Return to the Immigration office with the notification postcard, your passport, and current residence card. Pay the fee (4,000 yen revenue stamp). You receive your new card on the spot.
steps.tip: Buy the revenue stamp (shuunyuu inshi) at the post office or convenience store inside the Immigration building.

bring: Photo (4cm x 3cm, white background, taken within 3 months)
Supporting documents for your visa type

phrases.ja: 在留期間の更新をしたいです
phrases.en: I want to renew my period of stay
phrases.romaji: zairyuu kikan no koushin wo shitai desu

phrases.ja: 申請書はどこですか
phrases.en: Where are the application forms?

phrases.ja: 収入印紙はどこで買えますか
phrases.en: Where can I buy a revenue stamp?
phrases.romaji: shuunyuu inshi wa doko de kaemasu ka

title: How to Set Up Utilities
summary: Electricity, gas, water, and internet setup when moving into a new home

steps.title: Check what is already connected
steps.detail: In many Japanese apartments, electricity and water are already connected or easy to start. Gas almost always requires an in-person opening appointment. Your landlord or real estate agent should tell you which utility companies serve your address.
steps.tip: Ask your real estate agent for the utility company names and phone numbers before move-in day.

steps.title: Start electricity
steps.detail: Find the breaker box in your apartment and flip the main switch ON. If power does not come on, call the local electric company. In deregulated areas, you can choose your provider. Major companies: TEPCO (Tokyo), Kansai Electric (Osaka), Chubu Electric (Nagoya).
steps.tip: Many electric companies have online sign-up in English. You need your address and move-in date.

steps.title: Start water
steps.detail: Turn the water valve (usually outside near the meter) to open. Then call or go online to register with your local waterworks bureau (suidou kyoku). You need your address and move-in date.
steps.tip: Water is managed by the municipality, not private companies. Check your city website.

steps.title: Schedule gas opening
steps.detail: Call your gas company to schedule an opening appointment. An engineer must visit in person to turn on gas and check for leaks. You must be home during the appointment. Major companies: Tokyo Gas, Osaka Gas, Toho Gas.
steps.tip: Book the gas appointment before move-in if possible. Weekend appointments fill up quickly.

steps.title: Set up internet
steps.detail: Options include fiber (hikari), mobile Wi-Fi (pocket Wi-Fi), or home router plans. Fiber installation takes 2-4 weeks. Apply through NTT, au hikari, NURO, SoftBank, or other providers.
steps.tip: Check if your building has existing fiber. Mansion-type plans are cheaper and install faster.

steps.title: Set up payment methods
steps.detail: Utilities can be paid by bank transfer (kouza furikae), credit card, or convenience store payment slips. Automatic bank transfer often gives a small discount.
steps.tip: Set up automatic payment early to avoid missing bills. Convenience store payment is a backup.

phrases.ja: 電気を開始したいです
phrases.en: I want to start electricity

phrases.ja: ガスの開栓をお願いします
phrases.en: I would like to open my gas service

phrases.ja: 水道を使い始めたいです
phrases.en: I want to start water service

phrases.ja: 引っ越し日は〇月〇日です
phrases.en: My move-in date is...

title: How to Sort Garbage in Japan
summary: Garbage sorting rules, collection days, and common mistakes to avoid

steps.title: Get your local garbage guide
steps.detail: Every ward and city has its own garbage sorting rules and collection schedule. When you register at city hall or move into an apartment, you should receive a garbage sorting guide. Many are available in English from your city website.
steps.tip: Search '[your city/ward name] garbage sorting English' to find the PDF guide online.

steps.title: Learn the main categories
steps.detail: Most areas sort into: burnable garbage (moeru gomi) — food waste, paper, small wood items; non-burnable garbage (moenai gomi) — metals, ceramics, small appliances; recyclables (shigen gomi) — PET bottles, cans, glass bottles, newspapers, cardboard.
steps.tip: When in doubt, check the material symbol on the packaging. Japanese packaging is labeled with recycling codes.

steps.title: Learn your collection days
steps.detail: Each category is collected on specific days of the week. Burnable garbage is usually collected 2 times per week. Non-burnable and recyclables are typically collected 1-2 times per month. Put garbage out by 8:00 AM on collection day.
steps.tip: Your landlord or building manager can tell you the exact schedule. Collection calendars are posted at garbage stations.

steps.title: Use the correct bags
steps.detail: Some cities require designated paid garbage bags (shitei gomi bukuro), purchased at convenience stores or supermarkets. Other cities allow any transparent or semi-transparent bag.
steps.tip: Check if your city uses designated bags. Using the wrong bag means your garbage will not be collected.

steps.title: Prepare recyclables properly
steps.detail: Rinse PET bottles and remove caps and labels (these go in separate categories). Flatten cardboard. Tie newspapers with string. Rinse cans and glass bottles.
steps.tip: PET bottle caps are often collected separately as plastic (pura) waste.

steps.title: Handle oversized items
steps.detail: Large items (furniture, appliances over 30cm) are 'oversized garbage' (sodai gomi). You must call your city to schedule a pickup and purchase a disposal sticker. Fees range from 200-2,800 yen per item depending on size.
steps.tip: Some items (TVs, refrigerators, washing machines, air conditioners) cannot go as oversized garbage. These require recycling through the retailer or a designated collection point.

phrases.ja: ゴミの出し方を教えてください
phrases.en: Please tell me how to put out garbage
phrases.romaji: gomi no dashikata wo oshiete kudasai

phrases.ja: 粗大ゴミの申し込みをしたいです
phrases.en: I want to apply for oversized garbage pickup
phrases.romaji: sodai gomi no moushikomi wo shitai desu

phrases.ja: これは何ゴミですか
phrases.en: What category is this garbage?
phrases.romaji: kore wa nani gomi desu ka
